data.title: DFAR: A Reasonable Deal To Gain Indirect Exposure To US REITs
data.description:
Among the better REIT ETFs I've been researching this week is the Dimensional US Real Estate ETF (NYSEARCA:DFAR), a $1.3 billion fund that has nearly 50% comprised of the top REITs in North America across diverse categories. It is also one of the cheapest with an ER of 0.19%, which might be an important consideration for long-term investors. The ETF was incepted in February of 2022, and since then, it has not yielded any meaningful capital appreciation, and the distributions have been lumpy, with a current yield of 2.3% in the RRM period.

Investing in REITs vs REIT ETFs

REITs have always attracted income investors because they're mandated to pay out at least 90% of whatever taxable income is declared every tax year. That's the basic requirement of a REIT, among other ownership restrictions, and the way it's structured is that the trust owns the underlying real estate assets, but may choose to be managed either internally or externally. In general, internally managed REITs are more attractive because the managers can focus on just that one portfolio and can better align themselves with unitholder interests.

As an investor, you get to own pieces of prime property without directly investing in them. You get exposure to the distributions as well as capital appreciation of the assets over time. That's why REITs can be so compelling.

The Risks of REIT Investing: An Example

On the flip side, investing in a REIT can be risky, particularly when the REIT is non-diversified. Take the example of office REITs and what's been happening to them over the past few years. Ever since the pandemic hit, they've been struggling with declining FFO (funds from operation). Office REITs, which primarily invest in office-space properties, were somewhat protected by long-term leases, and they held on until around the end of 2021, which is when things started to unravel.

SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) as a good example. This New York office REIT was punished swiftly and severely by investors, even though it managed to keep its FFO relatively stable and even growing until the third quarter of 2021. The stock, however, has been struggling to get back to its pre-pandemic level, and that range-bound movement can be seen clearly in the price return chart below.

Remember, this is despite SLG showing impressive capital appreciation over the past year, delivering a price return of 145% since October 2023.

The only thing that allowed SLG to maintain its positive investor sentiment, in my opinion, is that it paid a couple of hefty special dividends in 2020 and 2021.

Moreover, management moved from quarterly dividend payouts to a monthly schedule, which gave them more flexibility without necessarily having to pay more in dividends. Unfortunately, the regular dividend kept dropping, from 31 cents a month (after the initial change from 90+ cents per quarter) to 27 cents in 2022 and 25 cents (past ten months.)

Investors can't be blamed for thinking this is a great investment opportunity right now, especially when the stock is trading at a sub-10x forward P/FFO, but if you'd held through the two of the toughest years since the pandemic hit, you'd be heaving a sigh of relief, at best. At worst, you'll be dreading that these wild swings in FFO persist beyond Q3, results for which were reported on October 16 to a lukewarm reaction from investors.

The reason I'm detailing what happened with SLG is that it is exposed to greater risk because its investments are concentrated in office properties, and what's more, this isn't the only REIT subtype to be hit by the pandemic. Retail REITs also took a nasty hit after the virus outbreak because consumers were no longer shopping physically due to the lockdowns.

The point here is that a REIT ETF is vastly different from a single REIT holding because it offers protection in the form of diversification. That doesn't mean your investment is 100% safe, of course, but that diversity ensures that your risk is spread across multiple REIT subsectors. Each of these subsectors has a major Achilles heel, so the more heels you have on the ground, the stronger your portfolio can stand against practically any attack other than a large-scale recession.

In many ways, a REIT ETF is also better than a diversified REIT because the portfolio managers have to be proficient in multiple areas of REIT investing, which dilutes their focus. That's not the case here - each holding of the ETF has its own portfolio managers who are highly experienced in those specific areas. Wouldn't you want your assets to be managed by specialists rather than Jacks-of-all-REITs? I would.

Another positive about REIT ETFs over standalone REITs is that even though you're not getting direct exposure to the individual REITs' distributions, you don't have to worry about constantly rebalancing your REIT portfolio to get the best gains out of the most well-performing subsectors. The fund manager does that for you, and in the case of DFAR, at a very nominal 0.19%.

What I Like About DFAR, Specifically

Let's get into the specifics of DFAR and why I think it's a good investment in the REIT ETF space.

One of the metrics I like to look at for any ETF is AUM growth, and it's important to do that in the context of price appreciation over that same duration. The reason is simple: it's easy to see why a fund might be attractive when things are going well and capital appreciation is happening in lockstep with asset growth, but what about DFAR, which has an abysmal price return of under 3% since inception? That's where the AUM growth really stands out. Of course, we're not discounting the organic NAV growth of the underlying REITs, but this type of steady addition to AUM is certainly encouraging. It implies that investor confidence in this REIT is both positive and stable.

The second major reason I like this REIT is the allocation to various REIT subsectors, so let's dig into that next.

A Brief Discussion on the DFAR ETF's Top Holdings

The ETF's top holding, Prologis, Inc. (PLD) is an industrial REIT and comprises 8% of the fund's assets. At a market cap of $120 billion, it is the largest of its kind, as well as one of the largest REITs overall. What I like is its historical AFFO growth rates in the range of around 8% to +9% over the past three to five years. Although analysts expect a slowdown in FY 2024, the next three years' estimates are between 8.4% and 11.3%, so even at the low end that would be around the historical average. That's good because it shows stability with the potential for some growth. With an FFO to Revenue ratio of roughly 70%, every dollar added to the top translates to 70 cents more at the bottom line.

What I like even more about DFAR's holdings is the 11% allocation to telecom tower REITs and 10% allocation to retail REITs. The former is comprised of a 7.5% weighting to American Tower Corporation (AMT) and 3.5% to Crown Castle Inc. (CCI), which together represent about $160 billion in market capitalization in this attractive REIT subsector.

The exciting part is that growth is almost inevitable in this sector. As more endpoint devices are deployed, the volume of wireless data will keep growing in a steady manner. The trends represented by this set of graphs from a little over a year ago are still very much valid and in play.

As two of the largest REITs in the cell-tower domain, both AMT and CCI will benefit not only from data and device proliferation, but also macro factors such as population growth and new technologies being brought to market at a rapid pace. We need faster and more reliable communication more than ever, and these REITs are a key component of the infrastructure on which that will be built.

The other allocation that, I think, is prudent is data center REITs, as represented in this portfolio by Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR). The combined $140 billion market cap positions these REITs well to capture the 10% CAGR projected over the rest of the decade. I suspect that doesn't factor in future workloads from AI, autonomous and connected vehicles, and other catalysts.

DFAR has weighted telecom tower and data center REITs at a combined 21%, which should support strong growth rates for the underlying assets over the medium to long term. This is balanced out by retail, self-storage, and healthcare, as well as some experiential and gaming REITs that aren't part of the Top Ten.

The undercurrent to all these REITs is the falling interest rate environment. I've said in many of my recent articles that I don't expect the Fed to keep making aggressive cuts, and I still stand by that; regardless, they'll keep coming down as and when inflationary and other economic indicators dictate their need to.

As an aside, a Democratic win in November might tilt things in favor of rapid cuts, but the Fed is not likely to give in to political pressure, in my opinion. The reason? The negative repercussions of rapid rate cuts now would be far too great on the economy, which is still resilient at the macroeconomic level but showing signs of distress at the microeconomic level; by that, I mean the bulk of consumers who are still burdened by the high cost of everything they touch - from staples too discretionary to credit.

In that scenario, though I might be painting a bleak picture for consumers, holding a REIT ETF like DFAR does make sense.

It's performed better than most of its peers (as selected by Seeking Alpha's default peers tool), has the lowest ER and much better liquidity than the majority of them, the lowest turnover ratio (attesting to the selection of its holdings), and among the highest yields in that group.

As such, I'm comfortable rating DFAR a long-term Buy, but with the caveat that there may be other gems in the REIT ETF space that I haven't come across yet. The much older JPMorgan Realty Income ETF (JPRE) looks a tad better from a price return perspective, but I'd need to do a full-on comparison to justify that assumption. For now, I'm happy to recommend DFAR to investors looking for indirect exposure to real estate. After all, when we're done with the dollar and gold and silver lose their shine, all we have is land. And maybe water, but we're not that far into the future yet!

Thanks for reading my work.

data.title: Archer Aviation: Dilution Risk Is Rising
data.description:
Archer Aviation's (NYSE:ACHR) share price has been under pressure in recent months, which could become a problem as a result of the company's capital requirements. Stellantis (STLA) has been sharing a fair portion of this burden, although likely expects to benefit through the manufacturing relationship. Stellantis has its own issues, though, and it is not clear how committed it will be if Archer's future begins to look shaky. Outside of funding needs and the risk of dilution, Archer's business is progressing steadily. I tend to think that Joby's (JOBY) lead in the space is widening, though.

I previously suggested that inflated expectations were a risk for Archer shareholders. While the company's share price has been volatile, it is currently down by around 7% since then. From a fundamental perspective, nothing has really changed in recent months, but the company's modest market capitalization and significant financing needs are becoming more of an issue.

Archer Business Updates

Recent regulatory milestones include:

  • The FAA finalizing Midnight's airworthiness criteria, enabling Archer to progress the type certification process.
  • Part 135 Air Carrier & Operator certification in June, which enables Archer to operate commercially in the US, once Midnight is type certified.

Archer is also nearing completion of for-credit testing of its composite airframe structural materials and environmental testing of system hardware. The company also continues to complete for-credit audits of various hardware and software elements of its aircraft.

In addition, Archer's certification efforts are progressing internationally, including in India, Korea, and the UAE. In particular, Archer believes that there could be an opportunity to deploy its aircraft in the UAE ahead of the US.

Archer conducted 50 flights in the fourth quarter of 2023 and over 100 flights were logged in Q1 2024. Archer more recently stated that it is ahead of schedule with its test flights, with the company completing 402 test flights so far this year, up from 230 flights at the end of the second quarter. This is a result of the company ramping up operations ahead of for-credit flight testing with the FAA, with the goal of demonstrating a commercial operations-like flight cadence (>15 flights per day).

Archer completed a transition flight with Midnight in June, flying at over 100 miles per hour. This is the second full-scale aircraft that Archer has achieved transition flight with. Archer plans on starting piloted flight testing this year to gather the data necessary to support type inspection authorization, after which FAA pilots will fly Archer's aircraft and perform for-credit flight testing.

Manufacturing

Manufacturing activity is still fairly muted at the moment, which is probably the result of capital constraints and Archer's testing timeline. Archer is already building its fleet of six aircraft that will be used for credit testing with the FAA. This is happening at Archer's California facility ahead of the construction of a larger facility in Georgia. Archer's high-volume manufacturing facility is expected to be completed by the end of this year, with an initial production rate of roughly one aircraft per month.

Archer has reached an agreement in principle on key terms of its planned contract manufacturing relationship with Stellantis. Prior to investing $55 million in July (upon Archer achieving the transition flight milestone), Stellantis had invested close to $250 million in Archer. Stellantis also plans on financing up to $390 million in manufacturing labor and CapEx. This is expected to cover planned manufacturing labor costs through 2030.

In return, Archer will issue shares to Stellantis on a quarterly basis, based on the costs incurred. Phase 1 of the relationship is expected to support production of up to 650 aircraft annually. Archer will also issue Stellantis $30 million of performance warrants that will vest based on the achievement of performance milestones.

Partnerships

Archer's ability to expand its relationships and order book continues to be the most positive part of the business. Archer recently signed an agreement with Signature Aviation for air taxi services, including the installation of rapid charging systems at terminals. Signature Aviation has the world's largest network of private aviation terminals. This is a potentially important relationship given that terminals will be one of the bottlenecks to scaling up supply.

Archer's planned air mobility network in the San Francisco Bay Area was strengthened by a memorandum of understanding with Kilroy Realty. Kilroy Realty's Kilroy Oyster Point campus in south San Francisco will provide a vertiport that connects to Archer's planned locations in Napa, San Jose, Oakland, and Livermore, where the company has existing relationships, including with Signature Aviation and Atlantic Aviation.

Archer also has plans for a Los Angeles air taxi network, including vertiports in locations like Los Angeles International Airport, USC, Orange County, Santa Monica, Hollywood Burbank, Long Beach, and Van Nuys. This network will involve partners like Signature and Atlantic Aviation and United and Southwest Airlines.

On the aircraft sale front, Archer recently signed an agreement with KakaoMobility, a Mobility-as-a-Service platform in Korea. KakaoMobility will purchase up to 50 of Archer's Midnight aircraft, with the goal of commercializing eVTOL operations in South Korea as soon as 2026.

Future Flight Global also plans on purchasing 116 aircraft and made an initial deposit when it signed the MOU. This represents a significant expansion of the company's order book and is the most positive recent development. Future Flight Global is expected to make significant pre-delivery payments later in the year when the definitive aircraft purchase agreement is signed. This could be meaningful as Archer has suggested down payments in the 1-2% range and pre-delivery payments of up to 50%.

Archer has a contract with the US DoD in 2023 with a maximum value of up to $142 million. As part of this, Archer recently delivered its first Midnight aircraft to the USAF to evaluate as part of its AFWERX Agility Prime contract. The next step is for AFWERX to conduct government-directed testing of the aircraft for the USAF and validate operational and military-specific mission concepts.

Financial Analysis

Archer's indicative order book is currently sitting at around $6 billion, which goes a long way toward justifying the company's current share price. Consideration needs to be given to the likelihood of this order book being fulfilled and over what timeframe, though.

Archer is targeting 40-50% gross margins, assuming 250 units produced annually with a $5 million ASP. Archer believes at this level of revenue (~$1.25 billion) it will also be able to achieve operating profitability, dependent on how much it chooses to invest in R&D and sales and marketing. This probably means that Archer will incur losses for at least something like the next 4 years.

Archer had $360 million in cash at the end of the second quarter, and subsequently added to this with a total of $230 million in equity capital raises. The company's free cash outflow has been sitting at around $100 million a quarter, though, meaning that this does not provide a particularly large runway (~18 months). Archer has suggested that its core expense base is currently sitting at around $75-80 million per quarter, with one-time expenses like materials and non-recurring engineering adding to costs.

Given Archer's arrangement with Stellantis and likely losses over the next few years, I would not be surprised to see Archer's share count end up at around 1 billion, undermining much of the company's potential returns.

Conclusion

Archer continues to target commercialization of Midnight within the next 18 months. Given the company's current order book, this could create significant shareholder value, if costs can be kept under control. Dilution is a large risk, though, which is compounded by the uncertainty that the certification process creates.

Outside of this, Joby appears to be better positioned. Joby's aircraft offer a higher top speed (200 mph versus 150 mph) and longer range (150 miles versus 100 miles). Joby also recently conducted a 523-mile hydrogen-electric flight. This type of capability could eventually enable eVTOL flights between destinations like Baltimore and Boston, or San Francisco and San Diego. The flight was performed by converting one of Joby's battery electric aircraft so that it could operate using hydrogen.

Joby's autonomy efforts have been boosted by the acquisition of Inras and Xwing. While this is more of a long-term initiative, Xwing's technology was recently used in the USAF's 'Agile Flag' exercise, with Joby's team recording more than 3,900 miles of autonomous flight between 9 military bases and public airports across California and Nevada. Autonomy will likely be a core capability for eVTOL companies for several reasons. It will help to ease the pilot bottleneck and lower prices to a point where demand is significantly higher.

Joby is also ahead on certification and manufacturing and has a much stronger balance sheet. As a result, the share price performance of the two companies has begun to diverge in recent months.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Vs. Goldman Sachs: Better Fixed Income Investment?
data.description:
In our previous analysis, we analyzed the stocks of The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (NYSE:GS), and Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) and concluded that Goldman Sachs outperforms Morgan Stanley in the investment banking segment, driven by its higher investment banking fees, brokerage revenue growth, and larger market share. In the asset management segment, Goldman Sachs surpasses Morgan Stanley with higher revenue growth and larger assets under management. However, when comparing financial ratios, Morgan Stanley demonstrates superior capital ratios and profitability. Taking all factors into account, despite Morgan Stanley's stronger financial metrics, we believed Goldman Sachs to be the superior company overall due to its dominance in investment banking, asset management and valuation.

In this analysis, we compare these companies’ fixed income securities to determine which company is better for fixed income investors. Both of these companies are highly similar, with their business models mainly encompassing investment banking and asset management, and are the top companies in these markets by market share. Firstly, we compare the two companies in terms of their fundamentals, based on the company’s segment performance and profitability focusing on their stability. Moreover, we compare the companies to determine which company has a stronger financial position based on key financial ratios. Finally, we compare the companies in terms of their fixed income securities ratings and coupon rates.

Fundamental Comparison

In the first section, we compare Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs to determine which company has better fundamentals for fixed income investors. Both companies are highly similar, with their businesses mainly focusing on investment banking and asset management.

As highlighted in our previous analysis, both companies have a strong market position, MS and GS are among the top companies by market share in IB and asset management and are well established in these markets. Thus, our focus on comparison to determine which is more suitable for fixed income investors focuses more on stability and growth track record.

Both companies have shown a positive growth track record over the years. In Investment Banking, the growth trend between Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley is fairly similar. When GS's IB revenues declined, so did MS, except in 2023, where MS posted slightly better growth at 1.2% compared to GS’s -1%. However, GS experienced a much larger revenue decline in 2022 (-31.5%) compared to MS’s decline of 16.6% in the same year. In 2016, GS revenues fell by 8.4%, while MS's drop was more moderate at 5%. The only year where MS underperformed GS was in 2019 when MS's revenues fell by 3.9% compared to GS's 1% decline. Despite GS having a higher average growth rate, MS is more stable, with smaller declines during downturns. Furthermore, GS Total IB Revenue has a higher standard deviation (19.6%) compared to MS Total IB Revenue (11.5%). Additionally, GS IB's revenue growth is more unstable than MS's due to a higher standard deviation, whereas GS AM's revenue growth is more stable due to a lower standard deviation.

In terms of Asset Management, the trend is also similar. GS’s revenues declined more than MS in 2016 and 2019, but in 2022, MS saw a slight decline of 1.9%, whereas GS experienced strong growth. MS's Asset Management segment has a higher average growth rate than GS. GS Asset Management Revenue has a lower standard deviation (8.5%) compared to MS Asset Management Revenue (12.9%), but this could be due to the surge in 2021 due to MS’ acquisition of Eaton Vance. More importantly, the average decline of GS IB is larger than MS IB, at -10.5% and -8.5% respectively, whereas the average decline of GS AM is larger than MS AM, at -7.0% and -1.3% respectively.

On average, the total coupon rate across all companies is 6.538%, with JPMorgan having the highest coupon rate at 7.674%, and UBS Group having the lowest at 4.979%. Credit ratings indicate the creditworthiness of these institutions, and a lower average credit rating score indicates better credit standing. Barclays PLC has the worst average credit rating score of 7.3, while JPMorgan and Bank of America have the best scores at 5.3. The coupon rate to credit rating score ratio measures the yield a company offers relative to its credit risk. In terms of this metric, JPMorgan stands out with the highest ratio of 1.44%, suggesting a better return relative to its credit risk.

Comparing GS and MS, in terms of the coupon rate to credit rating score ratio, which reflects compensation relative to risk, Morgan Stanley has a slightly better ratio at 1.109% compared to Goldman Sach’s 1.09%. This is mainly due to Morgan Stanley having a better credit rating score compared to Goldman Sachs. Moreover, based on the average bond seniority ranking, MS has the 2nd highest seniority bond ranking, whereas GS is ranked 6th, highlighting that MS has more senior bonds compared to GS. Additionally, having more senior bonds is better for fixed-income investors, as more senior bonds would mean investors will have “first claims on the company’s cash flows” and have first claim on “company’s property, plant, or equipment” in the event of bankruptcy or liquidation. Overall, despite the lower coupon rate to credit rating score ratio, Morgan Stanley holds a better average credit rating score of 5.7, compared to Goldman Sachs' score of 6.7, indicating that Morgan Stanley is viewed as less risky. According to Fitch, some of its key rating drivers mentioned include its robust capital adequacy ratios like the Tier 1 capital ratio and that “MS is well-placed to comply with the recently proposed Basel III Endgame rules”. Additionally, in terms of Basel 3 endgame impacting CET1, the Federal Reserve announced plans in September to increase “CET1 capital requirements for the G-SIBs by 9%”, which would increase capital requirements for GS and MS, as these banks are a part of (G-SIBs). As a result, banks “will be required to hold more capital against assets, which will reduce the size of their balance sheets”. Moreover, PWC highlighted that in terms of Basel 3 endgame capital implementation, it will “take effect on July 1, 2025 and have a three-year phase in of capital ratio impact until June 30, 2028”.

Additionally, another factor highlighted by Fitch is the consistency of its profitability as “operating profit as a share of RWAs was 2.6% in 1Q24 and 2.7% on average for the 10-year period from 2013-2022, comparing favorably to both U.S. and international peers”. In the first section, we highlighted the superior profitability of MS vs GS in terms of ROA, ROE and net interest margin which are higher than GS.

Moreover, Fitch had also highlighted that MS had a “diversified business model”, particularly around Wealth Management and Investment Management completed by its past acquisitions such as Eaton Vance and E*TRADE. Comparatively, Fitch noted diversification weakness in GS's business model, where they highlighted that “GS's ratings have been constrained by its business model's weighting toward investment banking and trading activities”.

In contrast, GS's focus on investment banking and trading activities indicates less diversified and more volatile revenue streams as highlighted in our first section where we analyzed the higher volatility as measured by the standard deviation of investment banking revenues compared to asset management. In terms of bond seniority rankings, MS has the 2nd highest bond seniority ranking, indicating it has more senior bonds compared to GS which is ranked 6th.

Risk: Lower Interest Rates

As highlighted in our analysis in JPMorgan, we believe one of the risks for Financials companies is the reduction in interest rates. In September, the Federal Reserve cut rates by 50 basis points and their projections indicate further rate cuts going into 2025. We believe lower rates could impact MS and GS' total revenues negatively. MS's net interest income was $8,803 mln or 16% of its 2023 revenues while GS's net interest income was $6,351 mln or 14% of its total revenues.

To conclude, we believe MS is the superior choice for fixed-income investors due to its stronger fundamentals and financial position. We find that MS demonstrates more stable revenue growth and smaller declines during downturns across key segments like Investment Banking, Trading, and Asset Management, with lower volatility compared to GS. Additionally, we observe that MS shows better profitability, with higher ROA and NIM, and a stronger capital adequacy ratio, indicating better capital resilience. We also believe that MS outperforms GS in asset quality, maintaining a lower percentage of Non-Performing Loans (NPL) and a more effective risk management strategy. While GS offers a slightly higher coupon rate to credit rating score ratio, we believe MS's stronger average credit rating score of 5.7 indicates lower credit risk. Furthermore, we see that MS's diversified revenue model, with a balanced mix of Investment Banking and Asset Management, contrasts with GS's heavier reliance on Investment Banking. Not to mention, both companies have average yield rate to credit rating score ratios above the average of top Financials firms. Thus, we rate both Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs fixed income securities as a Buy.

data.title: URA ETF: The AI Play Of 2025
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Global X Uranium ETF (NYSEARCA:URA) is a fund that is well-placed for the uranium bull market that is about to take off. The uranium thesis is straightforward.

Uranium (UXA:COM) is the fuel for nuclear reactors and Small Modular Reactors (“SMRs”). Furthermore, the demand for nuclear energy is rapidly starting to mount.

For my part, I've invested in Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC) and Centrus Energy Corp. (LEU). I run a concentrated portfolio, and I believe that I already have enough exposure to this overarching theme.

Without further ado, let's go to the underlying drivers of this exchange-traded fund.

Rapid Recap

A year ago, I concluded by saying,

I'm bullish on Global X Uranium. The rationale behind my optimism centers on nuclear energy's pivotal role as a complementary source of green energy. While renewables like solar and wind are crucial, they face limitations like intermittency and storage challenges.

Since that time, this holding has been rather volatile, even if in time, the ETF has for the most part kept up with the S&P 500 (SP500).

However, allow me to be clear, that back then, I couldn't have foreseen just how big a story this is set to become.

AI is the Driver of Uranium Demand

The growing interest in nuclear energy from major tech companies marks a significant shift towards adopting solutions to meet their rising energy demands sustainably.

  • Companies like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet, Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Oracle Corporation (ORCL), are recognizing nuclear power's potential to provide reliable, cheap, carbon-free electricity around the clock—an increasingly critical factor as data centers expand and their energy consumption intensifies.

This movement is highlighted by Microsoft's decision to revive the Three Mile Island nuclear plant, Amazon's acquisition of a nuclear-powered data center, and Oracle's ongoing work on designing data centers powered by Small Modular Reactors. These massive investments demonstrate a strategic alignment with nuclear's ability to provide a steady supply of energy independent of weather conditions, unlike wind or solar power.

In fact, just today yet another news team surfaces. This is the lead in that Bloomberg article:

An Nvidia-backed cloud services firm wants to build a new data center in Japan, with one prerequisite: There should be nuclear power available nearby.

This lead could not further epitomize this movement! And despite the consistent news flow of information, there are still wealth management investors saying that even though it's a good time to back uranium, uranium companies are still too volatile and tied to the uranium spot market.

Let me be crystal clear, tech giants are serious in their efforts to gather dependable nuclear energy as a robust part of their long-term strategy to mitigate the risks of fluctuating energy prices and grid instability.

Google’s consideration of nuclear sources for its operations and Microsoft's massive investment in utilizing nuclear energy underscore a growing trend: the tech industry is positioning itself as a significant stakeholder in the future of nuclear power.

On top of all that, I believe that SMR technology will be much more of a go-to solution than investors presently believe.

SMR technology operates as a much smaller nuclear reactor, which actually makes the energy a lot more scalable. Note, the SMR route was chosen by Amazon just two days ago.

I see nuclear technology becoming more scalable, which lowers the barriers for its growth and provides a way for tech companies to meet the increasing energy demands from AI-driven workloads.

For over 20 years, the U.S. energy usage has been stagnant, but now AI will cause a massive spike in demand. And this comes on top of consensus that carbon-based energy is harmful and something we should cut back on.

With that framework in mind, let's now discuss more about the URA ETF.

Why URA? Why Now?

This ETF is perfectly suited for investors who don't want the headache of trying to figure out the ultimate winner in this space.

As noted already, I believe that UEC and LEU are the best way to go. But that's just my opinion. For instance, it may turn out that Energy Fuels (UUUU) is the best play.

Without looking in hindsight, how does anyone even have a chance to pick out the best winner in what's likely to be a uranium bull market?

Investment Risks

The primary risk of investing in the uranium sector is the potential for a supply glut, which could drive prices down significantly. For example, Kazatomprom (OTC:NATKY) (listed in London under LSE:KAP), the world’s largest and lowest-cost uranium producer, could signal to the market its intention to increase its production if uranium prices rise too high.

This would flood the market with additional supply, putting downward pressure on prices and potentially hurting the profitability of other uranium miners.

That being said, Kazatomprom has already notified that it will be reducing its 2025 production.

The other investment risk is that the argument could be made that since there's already so much news flow coming to this space, there are a lot of eyeballs and capital chasing this space.

However, I would counter this by saying that many uranium miners, for example, UUUU, hasn't even returned to its all-time highs, even as the S&P 500 made all-time highs several times in the past month!

And this again echoes my contention, that backing an ETF could be for certain investors the best vehicle. After all, an expense ratio of 0.69% is perfectly reasonable for investors who want a “limited effort / maximum return” approach to the nuclear theme.

The Bottom Line

I believe that the Global X Uranium ETF is well-positioned to ride the uranium bull market.

With major tech giants like Microsoft and Amazon recognizing nuclear energy as a sustainable solution for their growing power needs, the demand is only set to increase. My portfolio is already heavily exposed to uranium companies, and while the volatility can be daunting, I remain optimistic about the long-term potential of this space.

Get ready for a nuclear ride!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Costco Is Running Out Of Gold, Here's What That Means For The Wholesaler
data.description:
Introduction

Costco Wholesale Corporation (NASDAQ:COST) is running out of gold, quickly. Last week, Bloomberg reported that 77% of outlets it surveyed had run out of stock of gold bars.

These little 1oz bullion are sold in small packages, picked directly off shelves by members, and bought for thousands of dollars on the spot. The price of gold has been on an absolute tear, and Costco is happy to cash in on it and sell this bullion off to its customers at a premium.

It seems that they can't stock these little gold bars fast enough to satiate demand, as they are now running out en masse.

Costco itself has actually outperformed gold in that same timeframe. Embarrassingly enough, all the gold bugs in Costco should've bought $COST instead.

Nevertheless, there are other reasons to buy gold other than appreciation, though that is arguably one of its most talked-about qualities. Costco itself has to invest in gold to hold inventory, meaning that it also poses some risk for them. This is why selling out isn't a bad thing; it is better to run out of a volatile asset than end up with excess and have to hope you sell it later.

What Does This Say About Costco?

It shows us a few things about Costco that I want to highlight:

  • Management is being risk-conscious, not hoarding gold in case of a crash
  • They are still making tremendous sales of gold
    • Wells Fargo estimates $200M/mo in gold sales
  • Costco has positioned itself favorably in the market, becoming one of the "good spots" among gold bugs

Costco, and wholesalers in general, are seen as favorably positioned for poor economies, as they offer shoppers savings and the ability to buy in bulk. This extends to gold, something also traditionally bought during times of economic uncertainty. It makes perfect sense for Costco to offer this, and customers are eating it up. All the while, Costco has been able to raise its profit margins this year.

It is still low, as that is part of Costco's business model, along with its famous loss-leaders the $1.50 hot dog and a drink combo and the $5 rotisserie chickens. However, rising margins are always a good thing, and the ability to generate larger profits from rising gold prices is a great boon for Costco.

This is one of the many reasons Costco itself has done so well lately, as economic data shows us the picture of a soft landing, but it is still uncertain.

Profit is Up Alongside Margins

Their revenue data has been very positive in the last five years, alongside their ability to manage expenses. They have been able to sell out more than gold, and have driven up net income by almost 180% in the last five years.

Despite an expected cyclical reduction in these figures coming in the next few months, it's likely that Costco can keep this trend up and continue to grow its revenue. It is still expanding to new markets, such as South Korea, Japan, Spain, and Mexico. There are at least four more US warehouses planned by the end of the year.

As of last month, Costco was operating over 850 locations across the world.

Valuation is Still the Greatest Risk

One thing that Costco is not, when it comes to its stock, is cheap. Running up like Costco has comes with a price: inflated valuations. COST is a great stock to cite when discussing the kinds of premiums US stocks can command.

A PE of 53, and a forward of almost 50 is incredible for a consumer staples stock. The average for the sector is under half this figure.

Despite this, COST seems to continue to run, but note that a recession that could help COST's business could hurt its stock due to its lofty valuation compared to its peers in the retail trade sector.

Of its peers, only MELI carries a higher PE ratio. Poor Target (TGT) is down at 16. Maybe they should start selling gold.

Consider Other Metrics

PE is not the only valuation metric to consider. COST is not necessarily cheap when we look at other measures of valuation either, although it does boast a lower PS than Amazon, but I assume that is due largely to Amazon's AWS component, which COST doesn't have a comparison for.

Comparing the top companies in its peer group, other metrics show us some variance.

Price to free cash flow is on par with WMT, which is a good thing to see, as FCF is one of the components I look for in a stock I want to own.

The per-share metric is very impressive for Costco and something investors may want to consider when looking at raw FCF metric, as it isn't shown when compared to larger corporations like WMT and AMZN.

Will the Gold Bugs Slow Down?

It seems unlikely that the trend will be bucked, unless there is some other catalyst. There is a worldwide gold bug phenomenon right now, with both consumers and nation states. China is currently at the forefront of this new gold rush, with young people now resorting to stocking gold beans since they can't afford the full ounce. The country itself is a massive importer of gold to its central bank as well. From 2022-2023, China increased its gold tonnage by a tremendous amount.

I think we can officially call a hard landing if Costco starts selling gold beans.

Central banks are buying up gold, with the latest reported quarterly changes in US dollar amounts showing significant increases and very few decreases in major trading nations.

While Costco isn't selling gold to central banks, this still influences the prices of gold, and increased central bank buying means that gold prices are likely to continue their upward trend. Demand is vastly outpacing supply currently in the consumer gold market, as shown by the 77% of Costco stores sold out in the survey done by Bloomberg linked at the beginning of this article.

This trend continually shows strength for COST and reinforces my confidence in the retailer to continue its upward trajectory.

Conclusion

Costco Wholesale is running out of products like gold, traditionally an investment hoarded during hard times. This is a signal for a few things, but one is that Costco is making a large profit from the new gold rush.

Now, it is doing so well that it is selling out of its gold en masse, something that is a boon for investors as it shows Costco's ability to manage volatile assets, generate demand, and become the darling for gold bugs at the current moment.

This, on top of solid fundamentals, leads me to rate Costco at a buy rating. I own shares of COST in my personal equity portfolio and recommend it as a holding for others looking to add a quality business to their equity portfolios. Aggressive investors should consider no more than a 5% allocation to COST in an equity portfolio, with conservative investors recommended to limit COST to under 3% of an equity portfolio.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Lithium Americas: This Surge Is Only The Beginning
data.description:
After years of underperformance due to oversupply issues and a sluggish economy, the lithium market is finally charging up again. I recently discussed why "It was time to go long" Arcadium Lithium (ALTM), and just weeks later, mining giant Rio Tinto (RIO) announced the stock's takeover.

The deal valued Arcadium Lithium at about $6.7B, which is cheap. This dynamic could be why Rio wanted the deal done quickly and quietly, as a seismic shift appears to materialize in the lithium market. Lithium prices will likely rise in future years as electric vehicles, "EVs," and other lithium-related demands recover and the transitory oversupply issues dissipate.

Lithium Americas Corp. (NYSE:LAC), is another exciting company in the recovering lithium industry. Recently, LAC received excellent news regarding its joint venture with General Motors (GM) at Thacker Pass. Also, LAC could see more positive developments as its DOE funding may be finalized soon.

LAC stock has been clobbered during the lithium downturn. Thus, its stock is relatively cheap, and given the positive developments in the lithium market, LAC's price will likely move much higher as we advance. Another critical factor is that lithium stocks should recover well before lithium prices, which is likely why the stock is moving here.

Technically - $2 Was Likely The Bottom

LAC became massively oversold during the drop in April and May, a decline that persisted into the summer and even the fall. However, the bottom may have occurred when the early August mini-crash happened, and LAC dropped to $2. About a month later, LAC retested the $2-2.25 support zone.

This appears like a long-term bottoming pattern, and the technical momentum seems to be shifting to a more favorable pace. The 20-day MA recently went above the 50-day MA, and we may see continued progress as the 50-day MA approaches the 200-day MA.

The momentum shift is critical as it impacts sentiment. As sentiment has been extremely poor for a long time, improved sentiment could drive this stock much higher as we advance. Moreover, positive news flow and sentiment are all LAC will have because the company will unlikely have any substantial sales until 2027 or 2028.

From a technical perspective, LAC could bounce around the $3-4 range before breaking out to the $5-7 level, potentially closing the gap. Crucial support remains around $2.80-2.50, and we can institute stop-out levels below support points, just in case. Despite the potential for more volatility and turbulence, the risk-reward ratio appears favorable in LAC.

What Makes Lithium Americas Special - Thacker Pass

The market may not care about what the company made today or yesterday. It's primarily about what a company can make tomorrow or a year from now, which puts LAC in a highly favorable place. The company received a conditional commitment for a $2.26B ATVM loan from the U.S. DOE earlier this year. Now that GM is "all-in" on Thacker Pass, it's likely only a matter of time before more constructive developments regarding the DOE loan are revealed.

For anyone unfamiliar with it, Thacker Pass is a massive project with North America's largest known and measured lithium reserves. LAC has exclusive rights to develop and operate the vast mine, which should provide approximately 40,000 tonnes of battery-quality lithium carbonate in phase one.

Therefore, LAC has an extraordinary opportunity to develop the Thacker Pass project provided the DOE and other loans and profit from the lithium production in the coming years. LAC's market cap has dwindled to around $700 million as the lithium route persists.

However, the Thacker Pass mine could be operating at full capacity (phase one) in 2028, enabling LAC to realize $1B+ in annual revenue as the initial phase of lithium production comes online. Phase two production is expected to yield double, approximately 80,000 tonnes of lithium carbonate annually, and the mine's life expectancy is estimated to be at least 40 years.

LAC's Estimates Could Be Low

Few analysts cover LAC's future earnings potential, and the estimates may be lowballed here. "Consensus" sales estimates are only about $135M in 2027 and $440M in 2028, presumably when Thacker Pass will be at full production (phase one). This seems highly pessimistic, and LAC sales could be much higher as market dynamics stabilize and improve.

Lithium Carbonate

Lithium carbonate prices have collapsed due to transitory oversupply and lower-than-anticipated demand for EVs. Moreover, the global slowdown and high interest rates have slowed various economic segments, heavily impacting the lithium space.

The price has cratered from a high of around 600,000 Yuan per tonne to just around 73,500 per tonne. If we translate to USD, it's like lithium crashing from about $85,000 a tonne to just about $10,000 here.

Future estimates for LAC and other lithium miners could be predicated on current rock-bottom lithium prices and likely don't factor in much room for growth. However, things could turn out much differently, as lithium carbonate prices could increase substantially in the coming years.

EV Demand Likely To Skyrocket Again

Whether EV demand will improve is a subject of some debate, but substantial growth will likely return. The automotive world is becoming more electrified daily, and many markets even have targets to phase out ICE sales. For instance, the UK plans to ban purely gasoline and diesel sales by 2030.

Denmark intends to ban ICE sales by 2030 and hybrid sales by 2035. Germany has approved a resolution to allow only emission-free cars on the road after 2030. Many countries, including the most significant car market globally, China (2040), have dates set to phase out ICE and, in some cases, hybrid vehicle sales.

Therefore, whether we like it or not, the world is going electric, and it will need much more lithium carbonate in the future. Global EV growth continues to be staggering, as sales as a percentage of new vehicle sales have increased from just 1% in 2017 to 18% in 2023.

U.S. EV Sales Likely To Surge

The U.S. is no exception, and despite the recent slowdown in growth, EVs should continue capturing more and more market share from aging ICE technology in future years.

EVs accounted for about 7% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. last year. This year, the percentage of EV unit sales will likely be close to 10% and may reach close to 5 million vehicles, or roughly 30%, by 2030. Therefore, the growth potential for lithium carbonate is immense. This dynamic is why we see GM securing its future lithium by partnering with LAC.

Moreover, LAC will likely make much more than its estimates imply in the coming years. Looking back on LAC estimates, the consensus estimate is around $440M, or roughly $11K per tonne for its anticipated 40,000 tonne production.

Really? Lithium carbonate is around $10K per tonne now, an 88% drop from the 2022 highs and back down to around the early post-coronavirus era when EV sales and sales, in general, were essentially dead. These analysts believe lithium carbonate will be at $11K in 2028. There is a bullish target for $600M in sales for 2028, suggesting the bullish case incorporates a price of roughly $15K per tonne, which seems very lowballed.

The Bottom Line: LAC Stock Likely Has Solid Upside Ahead

No one knows exactly where the price of lithium will be in 2028, as it's 3-4 years away. Nonetheless, markets have a tendency to correct themselves over time. Lithium prices were too low in 2020 as the EV rush began. However, lithium became overbought, and oversupply flooded the market in late 2022 and 2023.

After a prolonged two-year bear market, prices have probably become too low and may be much higher than $10-15K per tonne in three to four years. Even if we see a relatively modest recovery, lithium carbonate could be at about 200-250K Yuan per tonne in the next 2-3 years, in my view.

This dynamic suggests that prices for lithium carbonate could double or triple in a relatively short time frame. Therefore, if translated to dollar terms, a tonne of lithium carbonate could sell for approximately $25-30K in future years.

LAC's phase one production is expected to yield 40,000 tonnes of lithium carbonate in 2028. Using the midpoint of our lithium carbonite price estimate of $27.5K, LAC's revenues could be around $1.1B or higher as it enters full production capacity at phase one. If lithium carbonite is around $30K in 2028, LAC's sales could be $1.2B. If it's at around $50K per tonne in 2028, LAC's sales could be about $2B.

Also, there is likely a higher probability that lithium carbonate will be closer to $50K than $10K per tonne in several years. Therefore, I find the current LAC and other lithium minor projections absurd, as they seem to be predicated on a lithium carbonite price that will move up by 10-50% over the next 3-4 years when, in reality, prices could triple or more by then. LAC appears very underpriced here, and my one-year price target range for LAC is $5-6, almost 100% higher than its current price.

Risks to LAC

Of course, with great reward potential come significant risks. LAC is still in the development phase of the Thacker Pass project, and due to its immense size and scale, the development could take longer than expected. The costs could also be higher than we expect. Therefore, dilution or additional debt is possible in the future. LAC introduced 55M of new stock, pricing it at about $5, when it diluted shareholders earlier this year. This was a substantial increase in share count, and we want to avoid future dilution. Other risks may include unforeseeable issues at Thacker Pass, depressed lithium prices, worse-than-expected profitability, and other factors. Investors should consider these and other risks before investing in LAC.

data.title: Madison Dividend Income Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
For the third quarter, the Madison Dividend Income Fund (Class Y) returned +10.1%, which outperformed the S&P 500, Russell 1000 Value, and Lipper Equity Income peer group returns of +5.9%, +9.4% and +8.2%, respectively.

On a year-to-date basis, the Fund returned +9.5%, which compared to the S&P 500, Russell 1000 Value and Lipper Equity Income peer group returns of +22.1%, +16.7%, and +16.2%, respectively.

We wrote last quarter about the attractive investment opportunity in dividend stocks as many market participants were focused elsewhere on more expensive Technology holdings and the excitement surrounding artificial intelligence. Our view was that the rotation away from dividend stocks and into Technology stocks since the end of 2022 created an attractive opportunity to own high-quality above-average dividend stocks at below-average valuations. We maintain that view as many dividend stocks continue to trade at historically cheap valuations compared to the broad market. Our preferred valuation metric for dividend stocks is relative yield, which we define as a stock or portfolio dividend yield divided by the S&P 500 dividend yield. A high relative yield compared to its history indicates a low valuation for the stock or portfolio, while a low relative yield ratio indicates a high valuation, all else equal. At the end of the third quarter, the relative yield of the Fund was 2.1x the S&P 500, which was near the high end of its historical range since inception and 20% higher than the relative yield of the Fund at the end of 2022. In other words, by this measure the Dividend Income portfolio is 20% cheaper vs. the S&P 500 than it was seven quarters ago.

Our Approach To Investing – Relative Yield Strategy

The Madison Dividend Income Fund goal is to achieve long-term outperformance over a full market cycle while taking below-average risk. To pursue this objective, we employ a relative yield strategy where we buy stocks that have a dividend yield at least 1.1x the S&P 500. An attractive relative yield candidate is a stock with a relative yield near the high end of its historical range and a long dividend-paying history with a consistent record of dividend increases. Once we identify high relative yield stocks, we then analyze a company’s business model, balance sheet, and cash flow profile to assess its ability to continue paying dividends. We want to find stocks that have low valuations with potential for valuation multiple expansion while avoiding stocks that may have high dividend yields but face secular challenges.

This quarter we are highlighting Honeywell (HON) as a relative yield example in the Industrials sector. HON is a premier industrial conglomerate with leading businesses in commercial aerospace, industrial automation, energy sustainability solutions, and building automation. A common theme across these end markets is that HON embeds its products into the operations of its customers, which generates recurring revenue from aftermarket products/services and leads to high customer switching costs. We believe HON has a sustainable competitive advantage due to its wide installed base of equipment, strong reputation, pricing power, high customer switching costs, and technological expertise.

Our thesis on HON is that it appears well positioned for consistent growth, driven by a recovery in commercial aerospace following the Covid pandemic coupled with favorable secular trends in automation, remote security management and energy savings in buildings. The company made a strategic shift in recent years away from lumpy product sales towards connected systems and software, which lowers cyclicality and increases recurring revenues; we estimate that 30% of revenues are recurring in nature. This focus on software and recurring revenues has resulted in strong returns on invested capital (ROIC) over time that have easily exceeded the company’s weighted average cost of capital (WACC), something we expect to continue for years to come.

The fund originally purchased HON during the pandemic and added to the position in June 2024, after it reached a historically cheap relative valuation. As shown in the graph below, at the time of the most recent position increase, HON had a 2.3% dividend yield (top panel) and a relative dividend yield of 1.65x the S&P 500 (bottom panel), which was an all-time high. Based on this valuation metric, HON had never been cheaper compared to the overall market. The company has an A-rated balance sheet by Standard & Poor’s and is a Dividend Aristocrat that has increased its dividend each year since 1992. Over the past five years, HON has increased its dividend an average of 6% per year. We expect similar dividend increases in the future, which will help grow income and protect against inflation.

Dividend Yield and Relative Dividend Yield History for Honeywell

Summary

The Madison Dividend Income fund is a conservatively managed equity strategy that owns a high-quality portfolio of above-average dividend yield stocks with strong balance sheets and sustainable competitive advantages (wide moats). We believe that owning high-quality stocks with above-average dividend yields is the best way to provide income and generate attractive long-term returns over a full market cycle while limiting drawdowns in bear markets and market corrections.

While the Fund has trailed the indices in a strong market environment so far in 2024, valuations have now reached historically cheap levels compared to the broad market. Our view is that this provides a generational opportunity to own a high-quality portfolio of dividend stocks with attractive characteristics including:

▶ An absolute dividend yield of 2.8%

▶ Relative dividend yields of 2.1x and 1.35x vs. the S&P 500 and Russell 1000 Value indices, respectively. Both relative yield ratios are near the highest levels going back at least 25 years.

▶ A growing income stream that protects against inflation. Portfolio holdings have increased their dividends by nearly 8% on average over the past year, which is well above inflation rates over that time. Consistent annual dividend increases are a key metric we require in portfolio holdings.

▶ A high-quality portfolio with strong balance sheets that could protect on the downside in a market correction. To assess a company’s balance sheet strength, we look at its credit rating by Standard & Poor’s. 89% of fund holdings are rated A- or better, which compares favorably to the S&P 500 at 32% and the Russell 1000 Value at 20%. If there is a down market and/or credit spreads begin to widen, we believe our high-quality portfolio is likely to hold up much better than the overall market.

Sincerely,

John Brown, CFA & Drew Justman, CFA
